title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Credit Prices Drop WoW; Trading Activity Slows Down
content:
This WeekГўв‚¬в„ўs Highlights

(17th Гўв‚¬вЂњ 23rd November 2025)
  • A total of 239,716 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 7.24% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a decrease of 57.58% WoW as 782,351 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $52.00/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 2.07% fall WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers saw a fall of 2.83%, priced at $56.01/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 3.14% WoW; on 25th November, both the spot price and the benchmark price stood at $51.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 1.22% WoW. D4 prices fell by 1.00% WoW, while D5 and D6 prices remained unchanged WoW.

cCarbonГўв‚¬в„ўs Market View

The Renewable Fuels Association voiced its strong support for immediately expanding access to the lower-cost E15 fuel blend in California and urged the CARB to quickly clarify remaining questions regarding the immediate implementation of AB 30, while working in parallel to update state gasoline regulations to allow E15. In comments submitted to the California Air Resources Board, the Renewable Fuels Association recommended the agency require flex fuel vehicle capability in all new vehicles with internal combustion engines sold in California, at the earliest practical model year. The comments were submitted in response to a CARB Drive Forward Light-Duty Vehicle Program Workshop on October 21.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_608048" align="alignnone" width="777"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 2.07% WoW decrease, falling from $53.10 to $52.00 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices fell from $57.64 to $56.01, recording a fall of 2.83%. Type 1 trades witnessed 239,716 credits exchanged, recording a 7.24% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a decrease of 57.58% WoW, reaching 782,351 credits. [caption id="attachment_608049" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_608082" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 3.14% WoW, reaching $50.83/MT. On 25th November, the spot price stood at $51.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $51.00/MT on 25th November.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_608083" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 58 trades were reported in the market, with 5 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $80.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 20,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $44.00 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 15,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 100,000 credits for a Type 2 transaction, priced at $58.25. The smallest volume of transfers involved 1,400 credits for a Type 2 transaction priced at $45.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_608084" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $53.06 in November so far, falling from $53.80 in October. Trade volumes for the month of NovemberГ‚В stood at 3.32M as of 23rd November, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_608085" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 17th Гўв‚¬вЂњ23rd November 2025, total VPT fell by 1.93% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the Гўв‚¬Е“currencyГўв‚¬Вќ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 1.22% WoW. D4 prices fell by 1.00% WoW, while D5 and D6 prices remained unchanged WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesГ‚В 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Governor Kotek Issues Order to Cut Carbon Pollution, Accelerate Clean Energy Transition: Governor Tina Kotek signed Executive Order 25-29 to increase the pace and scale of OregonГўв‚¬в„ўs response to reducing carbon pollution while strengthening grid reliability and energy affordability. "Oregon faces escalating climate impacts, from record wildfires to extreme weather events that hit vulnerable communities hardest. Meanwhile, we must continue to make progress on our carbon pollution reduction goals and support reliable, affordable clean energy,Гўв‚¬Вќ Governor Kotek said. Гўв‚¬Е“This order outlines actions to reduce emissions through strategic electrification, efficiency improvements, and deployment of clean energy infrastructure that simultaneously fight climate change, strengthen grid reliability, and deliver more affordable energy for communities across our state."
  • RFA Urges CARB to Prioritize Immediate E15 Implementation: The Renewable Fuels Association voiced its strong support for immediately expanding access to the lower-cost E15 fuel blend in California. RFA urged CARB to quickly clarify remaining questions regarding the immediate implementation of AB 30, while working in parallel to update state gasoline regulations to allow E15. Гўв‚¬Е“The legislative intent of AB 30, which was signed into law on October 2, is crystal clear,Гўв‚¬Вќ wrote RFA President and CEO Geoff Cooper in response to an October 14 CARB scoping workshop. Гўв‚¬Е“The bill, which passed unanimously out of both the Assembly and Senate, authorizes E15 as a legal fuel in California, with the intent that retailers may immediately offer the fuel if they choose to do so. In addition, Governor Newsom has indicated the urgency of allowing California drivers to access E15, directing CARB more than one year ago to Гўв‚¬Лњprioritize resources that would allow for the expeditious completion of this process.Гўв‚¬в„ў Thus, to honor the statutory purpose and intent of AB 30 and the GovernorГўв‚¬в„ўs directive, RFA strongly encourages CARB to prioritize immediate implementation of AB 30 so that California fuel retailers can offer E15 as soon as possible.Гўв‚¬Вќ
  • RFA Unleashes Preliminary Agenda for 2026 National Ethanol Conference: S. renewable fuels industry leaders will gather at the 2026 National Ethanol Conference, themed "Ethanol Unleashed", to reflect on the robust growth potential that awaits, and the important work that still needs to be done to fully unleash ethanolГўв‚¬в„ўs potential. The annual event is presented by the Renewable Fuels Association and takes place February 24Гўв‚¬вЂњ26 in Orlando. Гўв‚¬Е“Once again, the National Ethanol Conference is being shaped by the priorities our attendees express each year, reflecting the direction of the ethanol industry as we look toward the year ahead,Гўв‚¬Вќ said RFA President and CEO Geoff Cooper. Гўв‚¬Е“As we move into 2026, several promising trends are emerging that give us strong confidence in ethanolГўв‚¬в„ўs future. Our aspirational theme captures the many potential paths before us as we enter what will be a pivotal year for renewable fuels and the rural communities we support.Гўв‚¬Вќ
  • Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) Tier 2 Pathways Release for Public Comment: The following pathways are being released for a 14-day public comment period: B082701: Fuel Producer: MARTINEZ RENEWABLES LLC (1845) ; Facility Name: MARTINEZ REFINERY (90001); Oceania sourced Animal Fat, pre-treated at various facilities, transported by truck and ocean tanker to Renewable Diesel plant in Martinez, California; Natural Gas and Grid Electricity; fuel produced in California [PROV3.0],B082703: Fuel Producer: MARTINEZ RENEWABLES LLC (1845); Facility Name: MARTINEZ REFINERY (90001); Oceania sourced Used Cooking Oil transported by ocean tanker to Renewable Diesel plant in Martinez, California; Natural Gas and Electricity; fuel produced in California [PROV3.0], B082704: Fuel Producer: MARTINEZ RENEWABLES LLC (1845); Facility Name: MARTINEZ REFINERY (90001); Oceania sourced Used Cooking Oil transported by ocean tanker to Renewable Diesel plant in Martinez, California; Natural Gas and Electricity; fuel produced in California [PROV3.0], B082705: Fuel Producer: MARTINEZ RENEWABLES LLC (1845); Facility Name: MARTINEZ REFINERY (90001); North American sourced Animal Fat, pre-treated at various facilities, transported by truck, rail, barge, and ocean tanker to Renewable Diesel plant in Martinez, California; Natural Gas and Grid Electricity; fuel produced in California.
  • RFA Recommends California Implement FFV Standard: In comments submitted to the California Air Resources Board, the Renewable Fuels Association recommended the agency require flex fuel vehicle capability in all new vehicles with internal combustion engines sold in California, at the earliest practical model year. The comments were submitted in response to a CARB Drive Forward Light-Duty Vehicle Program Workshop on October 21. Гўв‚¬Е“CARBГўв‚¬в„ўs number one guiding principle for the Drive Forward program, as stated in the staff presentation, Гўв‚¬Лњis to design stringent but flexible programs that achieve cost-effective emission reductions,Гўв‚¬в„ўГўв‚¬Вќ wrote RFA Chief Economist Scott Richman. Гўв‚¬Е“RFA believes that implementing a well-structured combination of requirements and incentives to grow the market for higher-level ethanol blends such as E85 in California would be among the most affordable ways to achieve significant reductions in criteria and greenhouse gas emissions from the stateГўв‚¬в„ўs light-duty vehicle population.Гўв‚¬Вќ
  • Oregon DEQ Takes Action to Ease Potential Fossil Fuel Supply Concerns: The Environmental Quality Commission approved the Oregon Department of Environmental QualityГўв‚¬в„ўs recommendation to grant program variances for fossil fuel suppliers that import fuels into Oregon outside the pipeline and terminal systems while fuel is not able to be brought into the state in the Olympic pipeline. The variances are a proactive measure to support continual fuel delivery through alternative methods in Oregon if the Olympic Pipeline closure continues.
